reviews.review_text: Better than 5, less engaging than 4, but overall I'd recommend it on sale. It's a solid city builder with some gameplay issues and an above average amount of stuff that feels tacked on or pointless, and the soundtrack in the whole series is stellar. I've never bothered with any of the DLC because none of it sounds like it adds enough to be worth the price, even on sale.

If i had only just reinstalled Tropico 4 again, i would have spared myself 50 bucks.
Everything after that one was complete ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥.
It smells a bit better than Tropico 5 though.

For the farm system alone i should be the one who getting money to play this pile of ♥♥♥♥.
You can't choose where the fields are planted, you can't choose where the farm building is set and you can't even mirror the placement.
That combined with the multiculture/soil degrading system is one of the most player hostile pieces of ♥♥♥♥ that i ever had the displease to witness in a game.

A random event that kills off 25% of your map-wide mining production and forbids tunneling on a sandbox map is nice as well.

Dear devteam, kindly GFY

If you've never played Tropico 6 before, it's kinda like Civilization VI strategy combined with Cities: Skylines building, except the satire is cranked up way past max, choombatta. If you like both of those, or even just Cities: Skylines, you should definitely give at least the basegame a try. Also there's character customization which is very cool.
I think it is a bit over hyped and not as good as some other city and colonial stratagy games.
A great game, easy to understand and play, with a nice sense of humour.   Developer is very pro-active.   I look forward to the next instalment,

I really enjoy this game. Much more challenging than your average city builder. It is rare to find games this hard.

I feel like how {-Insert Dictator Here-} must have felt when they sat down in the presidential office after overthrowing the government in a coup and realized "Wow, such great, fun power. I still need to do complicated politics and crap like that, but I have ultimate power now." I love it, I have so much fun running my own country exactly how I want it and having control over everything my citizens do. 8.5/10 , Great Game, would recommend, and I will buy Tropico 7. Note I have not played any DLC content.
Really fun relaxing gameplay. Tropical vibes give off real good comfort, get loss for hours in this game.
i think im too dumb to be a dictator (dont want to admit that i just dumb)... i kept failing this game.. omg ...

Tropico 6 is a city-builder, economy simulator, political satire, traffic-management nightmare, and mild dictatorship simulator all rolled into one suspiciously cheerful Caribbean package. You play as El Presidente, a leader so beloved that elections are technically optional, especially when the voters start developing dangerous ideas like “rights,” “wages,” and “not living next to a coal power plant.”

The game begins innocently enough. You build a few farms, some houses, a dock, maybe a chapel, and you think, “Look at me. I am a responsible leader.” Then, several hours later, you are juggling foreign superpowers, bribing political factions, stealing world monuments, building rum distilleries, ignoring environmental collapse, and wondering whether the rebels are angry because of poverty, oppression, or the fact that you placed their only grocery store on the other side of the island.

The best part of Tropico 6 is the balance between comedy and complexity. On the surface, it is colourful, silly, and full of ridiculous announcements. Underneath, it is a surprisingly layered management game. You need to think about production chains, trade routes, housing quality, job placement, education, healthcare, tourism, infrastructure, liberty, pollution, faction approval, and the economy. In other words, it slowly teaches you that running a country is difficult, and then gives you the option to solve most problems with propaganda.

The island system is also excellent. Instead of managing one landmass, you often manage multiple islands connected by bridges, boats, and questionable planning decisions. This adds variety and makes your nation feel more alive. It also means that when your economy collapses because all your workers live on Island A, work on Island B, shop on Island C, and die waiting for a bus somewhere between them, you can enjoy the full consequences of your own ambition.

The humour is classic Tropico: absurd, smug, and politically ridiculous. Penultimo, your loyal assistant, is basically the human form of a government press release. Every disaster is presented with cheerful incompetence, every questionable policy sounds patriotic, and every crisis feels like something that can be fixed with a speech, a statue, or another rum factory.

However, Tropico 6 is not perfect. The game can become repetitive once you understand the formula: build economy, satisfy factions, manage traffic, survive election, blame foreigners, repeat. Some systems look deeper than they actually are, and citizen behaviour can occasionally make you question whether your population has been educated or simply released into the wild. Traffic, in particular, can become your true enemy. Rebels are annoying, foreign powers are demanding, but nothing destroys a nation quite like 37 teamsters stuck behind one truck near a pineapple plantation.

The late game can also feel messy. Once your island becomes large, it can be difficult to diagnose exactly why everything is failing. Are people unhappy because of low wages? Bad housing? No healthcare? Religious neglect? Pollution? Lack of liberty? Or because they walked three miles to buy corn and gave up on democracy halfway there? Usually, the answer is yes.

Still, Tropico 6 is charming, addictive, and full of personality. It gives you enough depth to feel strategic, enough humour to stay entertaining, and enough chaos to ensure that every successful island is only two bad decisions away from becoming a Caribbean-themed administrative disaster.

This is a great city builder game. Very fun
Set population limit to 10k in the settings
Have played other installments and the writing is entertaining
Truly delightful city builder -- interesting gameplay, detailed supply chains, and a fun sense of humor come together over a banger soundtrack for a truly enjoyable playing experience!
The economics of the game seem off. A person can build industry, have businesses that are making money, yet your debt gets bigger and bigger.

For anyone who wants to learn basic economics, foreign affairs and international trades, this is THE game for you!
I started playing Tropico in 2001! 
My absolute favorite game.

Game has vastly improved from the past versions. Like the variety of the game type you can play. Do wish that the random maps would be a bit better. Seem to get the same maps frequently. Does not hinder the game but you pretty much know what to do. Like that the game does take to play, no rushing or quick game. Looking forward to Tropico 7.
Very nice city builder with production line and automation aspects, highly recommended. My only wish for Tropico 7 is that you are able to adjust the rotation of buildings and roads more.

Interesting supply chain improvements from prior iterations (I'd mostly played 1, 2, and 4) and lovely graphics.  I played during the pandemic to pretend I was on tropical vacation and it mostly worked for relaxing purposes.  I revisit the franchise every couple years and the urge to rule pixel people with a gentle iron fist has struck again :)
A really fun game. Love it, but once you crack it you crack it.
ahhhhh I Love this game so much I am completely addicted.
getting a job in real life really is much more fun
One of the most fun city builders I have ever played!
Finally, a Tropico to rival Tropico 4!
Better than Simcity and way more relaxing to play than tarkov
